chapters.title: Liberalism falters as disillusionment, work, liberty, and equality reshape politics
chapters.text:
Global politics is shifting visibly as technology and globalization strain liberalism's foundations.

After fascist Germany collapsed, liberalism emerged from World War II as one of two dominant governance models; communism eventually failed, leaving liberalism standing alone — yet now it too teeters.

Human rights, free markets, and democracy — liberalism's pillars — have weakened. Artificial intelligence, Big Data, blockchain, and cryptocurrency outpace governmental capacity to regulate information technology and biotechnology.

Economic growth tied to liberalism also accelerated ecological crisis; climate discourse grows more urgent. Compounding the shock: no clear successor ideology waits in the wings. Meanwhile, former champions of open markets retreat inward — American isolationism and Britain's Brexit exemplify the trend.

Western nations are not abandoning liberal principles wholesale; they increasingly want those principles domestically while rejecting globalization's demands.

You must understand that questions you cannot answer are usually far better for you than answers you cannot question.

Disruptive technology may eliminate many jobs, yet history suggests automation also creates new roles. Humans and machines could collaborate — but only if people continuously retrain and reinvent themselves to keep up with machines' cognitive capacity. Those who cannot adapt risk irrelevance when automation arrives.

chapters.title: As machines handle cognition, they may know us better than we know ourselves
chapters.text:
Delegating reasoning tasks to artificial intelligence will gradually increase trust in algorithmic judgment. Machines armed with vast data could manipulate decisions before we recognize the influence.

This prospect elevates ethics in AI development. Critics warn that machines lack conscience and follow code, not morality — yet algorithms execute exactly what programmers embed. Ethical failures trace to human design choices.

AI will not deviate from the rules embedded in its codes. Therefore, machines will ignore ethics only if their creators fail to include them in the source codes.

Power in the data age concentrates among those who control information flows. Major corporations already trade free services for personal data.

Public awareness grows, but effective policy lags. Most users prefer surrendering privacy to losing access.

Did you know? A Gallup poll found nearly 85% of workers worldwide dissatisfied with their jobs.

chapters.title: Communism, civilization, and nationality pose 21st-century political tests
chapters.text:
Following the 2016 U.S. election, Mark Zuckerberg pledged Facebook would rebuild communal bonds. Whether a profit-driven corporation can lead such a movement — potentially at revenue cost — remains unclear. Regardless of leadership, humanity must distinguish online interaction from offline community.

Social networks erased geographic barriers yet weakened neighborly connection. We track distant friends online while ignoring people next door.

Deteriorating face-to-face relationships impair cognition; many struggle to act without online validation.

Facebook may deploy AI to foster "meaningful communities," but success requires pulling attention back to physical reality. Globalization's benefits nonetheless persist: borders blur and political ideas converge toward a shared civilization.

chapters.title: Global integration accelerates growth while sidelining the less tech-literate
chapters.text:
Globalization will not erase manual labor or inevitably pit civilizations against each other. Instead, a unified civilization may integrate diverse labor models. Unlike premodern eras, nations increasingly face parallel challenges.

Yet America, Britain, and Russia cling to nationalism. In a world confronting shared crises, isolationism carries steep costs. Ecological degradation tops the list: 150 years of industrial activity destabilized the planet, and continued greenhouse emissions threaten catastrophe.

Nations must cooperate to govern technological excess and prevent weaponized machines.

Humanity poisons air, water, and ecosystems pursuing growth, driving extinctions across species and habitats.

Because environmental damage ignores borders, unilateral green policies cannot succeed. Coordinated global action is essential.

The same logic applies to disruptive technology. Automation and machine learning promise efficiency yet risk creating a "useless class," digital authoritarianism, and cyberwarfare. International standards for developing and deploying new technologies are urgently needed.

chapters.title: Religion and immigration will shape civilization's next chapter
chapters.text:
Nationalism's fractures connect to religion's evolving role. Science displaced clerics in domains once dominated by faith — health, agriculture, and daily problem-solving. Yet nationalists weaponize religion when cooperation depends on shared myths.

Japan's 1850s Shinto revival and modern Islamist governance illustrate how doctrine bolsters state loyalty.

Religion and ethnicity become identity anchors amid globalization's homogenizing pressure.

Religion has no solutions to the threats of nuclear annihilation, global warming, and disruptive technologies. Instead, it cements the principles of isolationism and stalls the values of liberalism.

Europe's immigration debate crystallizes the tension. Pro-immigration voices argue host countries under-assimilate newcomers; opponents claim immigrants resist cultural integration.

Missing are clear standards defining assimilation requirements, probation periods, and pathways to full citizenship. Host nations with strong cultural identities must accept that immigrants rarely erase their heritage entirely.

How Europe resolves these questions will influence liberalism's survival and humanity's capacity to confront globalization's largest threats.

chapters.title: Terrorism, war, humility, God, and secularism may ignite wider conflict
chapters.text:
Terrorism commands disproportionate fear and reactive policy from wealthy nations. The level of spectacle this topic attracts has placed it above even more frightening social vices that kill people in the millions. Sugar-related disease and air pollution kill far more people yet receive less attention. Fear of small militant groups has even motivated major powers to invade abroad, playing directly into terrorist recruitment strategies.

Harari recommends calmer counterterrorism: covert operations over brute force, dismantling networks quietly, and starving attacks of the media oxygen they require — potentially by reducing obsessive news coverage.

Success against today's terrorism shapes preparedness for nuclear, biological, and cyber threats tomorrow.

chapters.title: A third world war remains a credible danger
chapters.text:
Despite relative peace compared with historical norms, resurgent nationalism raises war fears. Some believe nationalistic views could ignite war, yet economic interdependence makes large-scale conflict costly — China, with a $21 trillion GDP, would risk enormous wealth seizing minor territory worth a fraction of that amount.

Recent conflicts suggest modern states wage war less effectively than medieval powers. Rational calculation discourages aggression — yet history warns against trusting human rationality entirely. Humility may be the antidote to stupidity.

Teaching humility grows difficult when cultures proclaim superiority of history, God, or nationality. Intolerance born of supremacy poses genuine danger.

The belief of one religion that it should dominate is a negative thinking that can lead to a full-blown war if not monitored.

Monotheistic doctrines asserting exclusive divine truth — and rejecting dissenters — intensify the risk.

What difference does it make to the dead, the orphans and the homeless, whether the mad destruction is wrought under the name of totalitarianism or in the holy name of liberty or democracy? ~ Mahatma Gandhi

chapters.title: Individual knowledge is thinner than we assume
chapters.text:
No person grasps world events alone; understanding always borrows from collective knowledge. Harari labels this groupthink or the knowledge illusion — a force that built modern civilization yet breeds blind spots.

Scientists fall prey too. Thus, scientists who believe that facts can change public opinion may themselves be the victims of scientific groupthink. Those believing facts alone sway public debate may underestimate how tribal loyalty overrides evidence.

Knowledge illusion enables unqualified leaders to hide behind party structures. Group consensus resists correction even when confronted with disproof — raising hard questions about discerning justice from injustice.

Acknowledging flawed understanding naturally unsettles moral certainty.

Truth grows too complex for simple ethical formulas. Actions in China ripple across Africa; ignorance is universal, demanding systems that excavate facts from noise.

Ironically, chasing comprehensive global truth often deepens confusion. Reality runs on narratives — scrutinize your information sources carefully.

chapters.title: Human resilience is the survival skill of the future
chapters.text:
Having glimpsed an uncertain future, prepare for rapid, unfamiliar change. Education systems designed for industrial stability cannot equip people for coming volatility.

This era promises to introduce disruptions at a rate that we have never seen before. Individuals must reinvent skills repeatedly or face obsolescence. Today's education system does not have the right tools and models to mold people that can survive the volatile terrain of the not-too-distant future.

Harari urges self-knowledge matching what corporations and governments already extract through data analysis.

Understanding your inner landscape helps resist manipulation and navigate upheaval.

Meaning cannot be imported wholesale from religion, nationalism, liberalism, or cultural myth. Harari found clarity through meditation — discovering the mind as chaotic and largely uncontrollable, with feelings arising from internal imbalance rather than external events alone.

Realizing this truth reframes problems and weakens external mind-hacking.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Truth is stranger than fiction, but it is because fiction is obliged to stick to possibilities; truth isn't. ~ Mark Twain

Predicting the next thirty years remains guesswork. Some have predicted a complete overhaul of current religion, government, and education systems. Yet credible alternatives remain elusive, so you will have to undergo a systemic reinvention that would match the rate of disruptions that tomorrow brings. Personal reinvention must match the pace of disruption; relevance depends on it.

Understanding your mind preserves agency as machines and states compete to influence thought. Seek authentic sources of meaning rather than inherited ideology.

Disappointment after failure stems not from abstract truth but biochemical reactions. Ridicule of faith or ideas likewise becomes feeling through mental interpretation.

Try this

Schools may not prepare you for what arrives. Cultivate resilience to adapt continuously and live meaningfully.

Support global climate policies — renewable energy, reduced fossil dependence, and reforestation.

Read it till the end?
